---
title: "fMRI Windowing"
subtitle: "Describe scan runs and query feature time without confusing it with native feature time."
sidebar: concepts
---

## The timing problem

An fMRI run has its own sampling schedule. The repetition time, or TR, is the
time between successive image volumes. A run with TR 2 seconds and five
volumes has sample times 0, 2, 4, 6, and 8 seconds relative to the start of
that run.

The same run may begin at 100 seconds on an experiment-wide clock. Its sample
times are then 100, 102, 104, 106, and 108 seconds on that clock. A stimulus
feature can use yet another origin. Windowing is reliable only when these
relationships are stated.

## `RunGrid` describes one run

A `RunGrid` stores the TR, number of volumes, experiment-wide start time, and
the names of the feature and experiment clocks. It also stores an explicit map
from feature time to experiment time. From these facts it derives a run clock
whose zero is the beginning of that run.

`ExperimentGrid` is a collection of `RunGrid` objects. The helper
`build_experiment_grid()` constructs both from common run-level inputs:

```{python}
from natural_features import build_experiment_grid

grid = build_experiment_grid(
    tr_s=2.0,
    n_trs_by_run=[5],
    run_starts_s=[100.0],
)
run = grid.get_run(1)

{
    "experiment_times_s": run.times_s.tolist(),
    "run_clock": str(run.run_clock),
    "run_end_s": run.end_s,
}
```

The older `feature_t0_s` and `feature_t0_by_run` arguments express a simple
offset between feature and experiment time. New code can supply full
`ClockMap` objects when the relationship also includes a scale difference.

## Two kinds of query

`query_feature_window()` selects the native feature rows that fall inside a
run window. It may express their coordinates on the feature, experiment, or
run-relative clock, but it does not change the number of rows merely to match
the scan TR.

`query_feature_window_tr()` performs an additional, explicit step: it samples
the selected feature onto the run's TR grid using the requested method, which
defaults to `mean`. This is a resampling operation, so the output row count and
values may differ from the native feature.

| `output_time` | Coordinates written on the result |
|---|---|
| `absolute` | Experiment-wide seconds |
| `run_relative` | Seconds from the beginning of the selected run |
| `feature` | The feature object's own clock |

The query window can likewise be interpreted relative to the run or as
absolute experiment time. Its end must be later than its start.

## Why these helpers are labeled compatibility helpers

The main `natural_features` contract now stops at lossless native-time
bookkeeping and `TemporalPayload`. New modeling code should usually receive
native feature rows and choose its TR sampling, interpolation, lags, and
hemodynamic response function (HRF) in the downstream analysis library.

The fMRI query helpers remain for existing workflows that deliberately want a
run slice or a TR-sampled feature. They do not estimate an HRF, convolve a
feature with one, or build a design matrix.

Unknown run numbers, non-positive TRs or run lengths, inconsistent clock-map
endpoints, invalid output-time names, and windows whose end does not follow
their start are rejected.

## See also

[Query a run-aware fMRI window](../cookbook/fmri-window-query.qmd) · [Native time and clocks](native-time-and-clocks.qmd) · [fMRI querying reference](../reference/query_feature_window_tr.qmd)
